posts.title: Role velocity as a timing signal
posts.hook: built a clay workflow last week that takes 2000 linkedin company urls and returns a qualified account list in 12 minutes.
posts.body:
built a clay workflow last week that takes 2000 linkedin company urls and returns a qualified account list in 12 minutes.

the stack:
- pull company data via linkedin enrichment
- hit crunchbase for funding signals
- use an http module to query their careers page
- claude prompt scores role velocity on a 1-5 scale
- filter to accounts where role velocity > 3 AND funding < 18 months old

total cost per row came in at $0.14. full list ran $280.

clay is powerful. that's not news. the useful bit is that role velocity (how fast they're hiring into a specific function) predicts buying readiness better than any intent signal i've tested. companies don't buy tools. they buy tools because someone new walks in and needs to solve a problem.

if you're building lists on technographic fit alone, you're missing the timing layer. role velocity gives you the when. fit gives you the who.

dropping the full workflow in the comments including the claude prompt and the column formulas. no gatekeeping.

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: most account scoring is theater. the only signal that actually predicts a deal is whether they're hiring fast enough to need you.
remixes.body:
most account scoring is theater. fit scores, intent data, technographics. all of it correlates weakly with close rate.

the one signal that consistently predicts a buying window: role velocity. how fast is this company adding headcount in the function we sell to.

built a clay workflow last week to test the theory at scale. 2000 linkedin urls in, qualified list out in 12 minutes.

the filter that mattered was not industry, not revenue band, not tech stack. it was role velocity above 3 (meaning 3+ relevant hires in the last 90 days) combined with funding under 18 months old.

that slice converted to meeting at 4x the rate of the broader list. same outbound copy, same SDRs. the only variable that moved was who we picked.

stop scoring on what companies look like. start scoring on what they're doing right now.
remixes.cta: what signal in your stack would survive being the only signal you used?

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: here's the exact 12-minute clay workflow that turns 2000 linkedin company urls into a ranked account list.
remixes.body:
five steps. one workflow. zero analysts.

step 1: drop the urls into a clay table. enrich with linkedin company data. headcount, growth, industry.

step 2: chain a crunchbase lookup. last funding round, amount, date. flag anything older than 18 months.

step 3: use the http module to hit each company's careers page. count open roles. filter to the function you sell to.

step 4: pass the role count plus the last 90 days of new hires to a claude prompt. score role velocity 1-5. anchor the rubric in the prompt so scores stay consistent across runs.

step 5: filter rows where role velocity is above 3 and funding is fresh.

cost per run lands around 18 cents in claude credits plus the clay enrichment line item. for a list of 2000, that is the price of one cold dial in pipeline terms.

the analyst who used to do this in a week is now doing the work that actually requires judgment.
remixes.cta: what part of your scoring still needs a human in the loop?

tones.advisor:
if you're still scoring accounts on static fit data, you're a generation behind.

here's what i recommend: build a workflow that combines two dynamic signals before any static ones touch the score.

role velocity, measured as net new hires in the function you sell to over the last 90 days. funding recency, measured in months since last raise. those two together predict buying windows better than any firmographic cut i've tested.

the build is straightforward in clay. linkedin enrichment for headcount and roles. crunchbase for funding. an http module against the careers page for live role counts. a claude prompt to normalize role velocity onto a 1-5 scale. then filter on velocity above 3 and funding inside 18 months.

i ran 2000 company urls through this last week. 12 minutes. roughly 20 cents per 1000 in model cost.

the accounts that survived the filter converted at multiples of the broader list. the takeaway for any RevOps leader: your scoring model should refresh weekly, not quarterly, because the signals that matter are kinetic.

meanwhile the only signal that actually predicts whether a company will buy is sitting on their careers page in plain text.

role velocity. how many people are they hiring into the function you sell to, right now. combine it with funding under 18 months old and you have a real-time buying window detector.

built the workflow in clay last week. 2000 urls in, qualified list out in 12 minutes. linkedin enrichment, crunchbase, an http module against careers pages, claude scoring on a 1-5 rubric. cost was lunch money.

the results made our previous scoring model look like astrology. accounts that cleared the velocity filter converted at multiples of the rest. same SDRs, same copy, dramatically different outcomes.

if your scoring refreshes quarterly, you are bringing a quarterly tool to a weekly fight. the buyers your competitors are closing this month were invisible to your model in january.

posts.title: RevOps is quietly eating three jobs
posts.hook: the revops title is quietly eating three jobs at once and most leaders haven't caught up yet.
posts.body:
the revops title is quietly eating three jobs at once and most leaders haven't caught up yet.

five years ago revops was sales ops with a fancier name. reporting, forecasting, salesforce admin work. a support function.

now the revops people i know are shipping:
- lead scoring models in python
- enrichment pipelines in clay
- attribution models that actually work
- slack bots that route deals
- data warehouses that unify hubspot + segment + stripe

they're not operating the stack anymore. they're engineering it.

the revops role is absorbing three separate functions: sales ops, marketing ops, and data engineering. tools got cheap enough that one person can do what required three teams in 2020. clay + segment + a claude api key + basic python collapse an entire stack.

leadership still posts revops jobs with jira boards and salesforce workflows in the requirements. meanwhile the actual revops people are shipping production code.

if you're hiring for revops in 2026 and asking about salesforce certifications instead of python fluency, you're going to hire the wrong person for what the job actually is.

what does your job description say vs what you actually do day-to-day?
posts.cta: what does your job description say vs what you actually do day-to-day?

inspired_by.summary: Shares insights on revenue operations as the backbone of high-growth SaaS companies. Details how RevOps unifies sales, marketing, and customer success through shared KPIs and tech stacks. Includes case studies from scaling startups.

remixes.label: job-evolution
remixes.hook: the modern revops job description is three jobs in a trench coat and the comp bands haven't caught up.
remixes.body:
five years ago revops was sales ops with a fancier name. salesforce admin, forecast hygiene, board deck data. a support function that reported into the CRO.

the revops people i actually work with today are doing three jobs simultaneously.

job one is still systems of record. CRM, attribution, forecasting, the stuff the org thinks of as 'revops.' that's table stakes now.

job two is data engineering. python pipelines, clay workflows, enrichment APIs, scoring models. the work used to live in a separate analytics team. now it sits with the revops lead because the iteration loop is too tight to hand off.

job three is product. they are building internal tools. slack bots that route deals, agents that draft follow-ups, dashboards that get used. they ship software.

the leaders who haven't noticed are paying senior engineer prices for a role they still benchmark against sales ops bands. that gap is where the best revops people are quietly leaving for ops-engineer titles at companies that figured it out.
remixes.cta: is your revops lead being comped for the job they're doing or the job in the title?

remixes.label: hiring-implications
remixes.hook: if you're hiring a head of revops in 2026 and the job description still says 'salesforce administration experience required,' you're hiring for 2018.
remixes.body:
the role has fundamentally shifted. the best revops candidates i talk to every week are not coming from sales ops backgrounds anymore.

they are coming from data engineering, internal tools teams, growth engineering. they have python and SQL as a baseline. they've shipped at least one internal product that the GTM org uses daily. they think in pipelines, not pivot tables.

the job they actually do now: own the data layer end to end, build the enrichment and scoring stack, ship internal tools that compress the SDR and AE workflow, and yes, also keep the CRM honest.

if your job description leads with administration and reporting, the people qualified for the modern version of this role will scroll past it.

three changes worth making before your next req goes live: lead with the building, not the maintaining. require shipping evidence, not certifications. benchmark comp against senior engineer bands, not ops bands.

posts.title: Reply agent voice dna lesson
posts.hook: spent saturday building an agent that reads my inbound linkedin messages and drafts responses in my voice. it shipped. it also embarrassed me immediately.
posts.body:
spent saturday building an agent that reads my inbound linkedin messages and drafts responses in my voice. it shipped. it also embarrassed me immediately.

the stack:
- playwright scrapes my linkedin inbox every 30 mins
- conversations get chunked and embedded
- claude sonnet drafts a reply using a voice dna file built from 300 of my past messages
- drafts queue in a slack channel for me to approve or edit

first real test: someone asked if i was open to a consulting gig. agent drafted 'thanks for reaching out! i'd love to learn more about the opportunity.'

i have never said that sentence in my life. the voice dna file only had public post data. my linkedin dms are shorter, more direct, more casual than my posts.

rebuilt the voice file with 300 actual dm examples. drafts started sounding like me. the lesson: agents inherit whatever context you feed them. i fed it my public voice and it produced public voice responses for private conversations. two different voices.

if you're building agents that speak for you, you need separate voice dna for each surface. public posts, dms, emails, slack. they are not the same voice.

full prompt and voice dna structure in the comments.

remixes.label: failure-mode
remixes.hook: my voice cloning agent failed in a way i didn't expect. it nailed the words. it missed the timing.
remixes.body:
built a reply agent for my linkedin inbox last weekend. playwright scrapes every 30 minutes, conversations get embedded, claude sonnet drafts a response using a voice DNA file built from 300 of my past messages. drafts queue in slack for approval.

the word-level voice match was eerily good. cadence, lowercase energy, the way i hedge before a strong opinion. all there.

what broke was timing. the agent treated every message as a flat priority. a recruiter pinging me, a partner asking about a deliverable, and a friend dropping a meme all generated drafts at the same urgency. the model has no concept of relationship state, no memory of what we last shipped together, no read on whether someone is mid-deal or just saying hi.

the voice DNA file solved the easy half of the problem. the hard half is everything that lives outside the words. when to reply, when to wait, when to send a voice memo instead.

rolling in two changes this week: a relationship graph that tags every contact with deal stage and last-touch context, and a 'do not draft' filter for anyone i'd respond to with a single emoji.
remixes.cta: if you've shipped a reply agent, what was the second thing that broke after you got the voice right?

remixes.label: build-vs-buy
remixes.hook: considered buying a voice cloning tool. spent saturday building one instead. here's why the build won by hour three.
remixes.body:
the off-the-shelf options all promise the same thing. paste in some examples, generate replies in your style, save hours per week.

every one i tested produced a flattened, polite, slightly american version of me. the voice in the output was 'professional creator.' it was no one.

so i built. playwright pulls my linkedin inbox every 30 minutes. conversations get chunked and embedded against a voice DNA file built from 300 of my own messages. claude sonnet drafts. drafts queue in slack so nothing ships without me reading it.

three things the build gave me that the buy never could.

first, the voice DNA file is mine. 300 messages of context, including the failure modes, the times i was too sharp, the times i hedged. the model learns the full surface area, not the marketing version.

second, the queue is local. nothing in someone else's database. my inbound is sensitive enough that this matters.

third, i can change the prompt at 11pm on a sunday when i notice the drafts are off. no support ticket, no roadmap.

the agent embarrassed me twice in the first week, both times in ways i could fix in 20 minutes.
remixes.cta: what's a tool you almost bought but built instead, and was the build worth it?

tones.advisor:
if you're considering building a voice cloning reply agent, here's what i recommend you scope before writing a line of code.

start with the voice DNA file. pull at least 300 of your own past messages, ideally a mix of cold replies, warm replies, and ones you'd consider failures. that range matters. a model trained only on your best messages will sound like a curated version of you, which is what every off-the-shelf tool already does badly.

next, decide your draft surface. mine queues in slack so nothing ships without my eyes on it. that single design choice is what turned an embarrassing first week into a useful tool. autonomy comes after trust, not before.

then, plan for the second failure. the words will be the easy part. the hard part is timing, relationship context, and knowing when not to reply at all. budget at least as much time for those layers as for the voice work itself.

stack i landed on: playwright for inbox scraping every 30 minutes, embeddings against the voice DNA file, claude sonnet for drafting, slack for the approval queue. total build time was a saturday. total iteration time is ongoing and worth it.
tones.provocateur:
every founder building a 'voice agent' is solving the wrong half of the problem.

the voice itself is easy. embed 300 of your past messages, prompt claude well, and the cadence will land. that's a saturday afternoon.

the hard part, the part nobody on linkedin is posting about, is everything that lives outside the words. timing, relationship state, deal context, the difference between a partner pinging you mid-deal and a recruiter saying hi.

built the agent saturday. shipped it. it embarrassed me by tuesday.

not because the words were wrong. they were uncannily right. it embarrassed me because it treated a friend dropping a meme with the same drafting energy as a partner asking about a deliverable. the model has no memory of who you are to each other.

the AI agents space is full of demos that solve the voice problem and pretend the rest doesn't exist. it does. and the people shipping reply agents into prod without a relationship graph are about to learn this in public.

if your agent doesn't know what stage your deal is in, it shouldn't be drafting your replies.

posts.title: AI SDR tools, data is the moat
posts.hook: every ai sdr tool i've tested in the last 6 months has the same problem. the output is fine. the inputs are garbage.
posts.body:
every ai sdr tool i've tested in the last 6 months has the same problem. the output is fine. the inputs are garbage.

11x. regie.ai. clay agents. artisan. the demos all look the same: type in an icp, the agent researches the company, drafts a personalized email, sends at the right time.

the demo works because the demo account is a fortune 500 with 10 years of linkedin data, a polished website, and 47 press mentions. the agent has everything it needs to write something decent.

your actual pipeline isn't fortune 500. it's 500-person manufacturing companies with outdated websites, no blog, and a ceo who hasn't posted on linkedin since 2019. the agent writes 'i noticed you've been growing your operations' because that's all the signal it can find.

the bottleneck is never the agent. it's the data layer feeding the agent.

before you buy another ai sdr tool, ask: what enrichment sources do i have that my competitors don't? what proprietary signals am i feeding in? if the answer is 'we use apollo like everyone else' then any agent you buy produces the same mid output as everyone else's agent.

the moat is the data. the agent is the commodity.

what proprietary signals are you building into your stack?
posts.cta: what proprietary signals are you building into your stack?

inspired_by.author: Eliot Peper
inspired_by.summary: Peper reflects on the hype vs. reality of AI agents, critiquing current developer tools and calling for robust infrastructure to enable true autonomy. He outlines a roadmap for automation in enterprise settings. Draws from recent benchmarks showing agent limitations.

remixes.label: data-moat
remixes.hook: the AI SDR category isn't competing on AI. it's competing on data. the tools shipping the best emails have the worst-kept secret: they're using your enrichment, not their model.
remixes.body:
every ai sdr tool has converged on the same architecture. pull a company, research it, draft a personalized email, send at the right time. the model layer is a commodity. the prompts are public. the workflows are nearly identical.

the variable that decides which tool produces a useful email is the data going in. and the data going in is almost entirely yours.

the demo always works because the demo company is a fortune 500 with a decade of public LinkedIn data, a polished website, a press page, and a tagged pricing model. the model has plenty to chew on.

your actual ICP is a 80-person company in industrial logistics with a one-page website and a CEO who hasn't posted in 18 months. the model has nothing. so it generates a polite, generic, slightly off-key email.

the moat is not the AI. the moat is the proprietary data you bring to the AI. your call recordings. your past won deals. your support tickets. your win/loss notes. your customer panel quotes.

the teams winning with AI sdr in 2026 are spending 80% of their build time on the data layer and 20% on the agent. the teams losing are doing the inverse.
remixes.cta: what proprietary data are you feeding your outbound stack that your competitors can't replicate?

remixes.label: category-prediction
remixes.hook: the AI SDR category is going to collapse into two camps in the next 12 months. one will eat the other.
remixes.body:
camp one: thin agent wrappers. 11x, regie, artisan, the long tail of clay agents. they all do the same thing with slightly different UI. they will compete on price and lose margin to zero.

camp two: data layer plus agent. the tools that own the enrichment pipeline, the relationship graph, the conversation memory, and use the agent as the last-mile interface. these will quietly absorb the category.

the reason camp one is doomed is that the agent layer has no defensibility. anyone with a claude key and a weekend can rebuild the workflow. the demo looks impressive in a controlled environment. it falls apart on a real account that doesn't have a decade of LinkedIn breadcrumbs.

camp two is harder to start, harder to copy, and easier to defend. the moat is in the data, the integrations, the feedback loops from won and lost deals. you can't replicate that with a fresh prompt.

the practical implication for buyers right now: don't subscribe to a tool whose value evaporates the moment a competitor ships the same workflow. subscribe to tools that get smarter with your data, not just faster with it.
remixes.cta: which AI sdr tool in your stack would you keep if a free version of the same workflow shipped tomorrow?

tones.builder:
every ai sdr tool i've tested in the last 6 months has the same problem. the output is fine. the inputs are garbage.

11x. regie.ai. clay agents. artisan. the demos all look the same: type in an icp, the agent researches the company, drafts a personalized email, sends at the right time.

the demo works because the demo account is a fortune 500 with 10 years of linkedin data, a polished website, and 47 press mentions. the agent has everything it needs to write something decent.

your actual pipeline isn't fortune 500. it's 500-person manufacturing companies with outdated websites, no blog, and a ceo who hasn't posted on linkedin since 2019. the agent writes 'i noticed you've been growing your operations' because that's all the signal it can find.

the bottleneck is never the agent. it's the data layer feeding the agent.

before you buy another ai sdr tool, ask: what enrichment sources do i have that my competitors don't? what proprietary signals am i feeding in? if the answer is 'we use apollo like everyone else' then any agent you buy produces the same mid output as everyone else's agent.

the moat is the data. the agent is the commodity.

what proprietary signals are you building into your stack?
tones.advisor:
if you're evaluating ai sdr tools right now, here's the recommendation i'd give a CRO this quarter.

the model layer is a commodity. every tool in the category is built on the same handful of foundation models, with prompts that are public knowledge and workflows that look interchangeable. the demos all work because the demo company is a fortune 500 with a decade of public data to draw from.

the variable that decides whether the tool works for your business is the data going in. specifically, your proprietary data. call recordings, win/loss notes, support tickets, past won deals, customer panel quotes. the kind of input no vendor has access to until you give it to them.

the purchase decision should reframe around three questions. one, can the tool ingest my proprietary data structurally, not as a prompt afterthought. two, does the output get measurably better as i feed it more of that data, or does it plateau. three, who owns the data and the workflows if i churn off the tool in 12 months.

the vendors that can answer those three honestly are the ones worth paying. the rest are renting you a workflow you could rebuild in clay over a weekend.

the entire ai sdr category is a magic trick.

the demo works because the demo account is a fortune 500 with ten years of public LinkedIn breadcrumbs, a polished website, a press page, and a CMO who tweets daily. the model has a buffet to work with. of course the email looks personalized.

your actual ICP is a 60-person logistics company whose CEO hasn't posted since 2022. you point the same tool at them and it generates a politely generic email that anyone with a chatgpt subscription could have written in two minutes.

the AI is fine. the AI is great, even. the inputs are the problem, and the inputs are almost entirely yours. your call recordings, your win notes, your past deals, your customer language. the data nobody else has.

so when a vendor sells you 'AI sdr,' what they're really selling you is a wrapper that looks impressive in their controlled demo and falls apart on your actual book.

the teams winning aren't buying these tools. they're building the data layer first and using the model as the last-mile renderer.

posts.title: Why we killed outreach
posts.hook: killed our outreach subscription 4 months ago. replaced it with instantly + clay + a zapier loop. sending 3x the volume, 40% better deliverability, 60% less cost.
posts.body:
killed our outreach subscription 4 months ago. replaced it with instantly + clay + a zapier loop. sending 3x the volume, 40% better deliverability, 60% less cost.

the actual math:
- outreach: $180 per seat per month, 3 seats = $540/mo
- instantly: $97/mo for unlimited sending
- clay: $349/mo for enrichment and list building
- zapier: $50/mo for the plumbing
- new stack total: $496/mo

that's not the story. the story is deliverability.

outreach funnels everything through their shared infrastructure. your sending reputation is tied to whatever else is happening on their stack. last year we watched our reply rate drop 3 weeks straight. support couldn't explain it.

instantly lets you bring your own mailboxes, warm them individually, rotate domains, and monitor deliverability at the inbox level. we went from 32% open rate to 58% after the migration. same list and copy, different infrastructure.

the lesson: the name brand tool is optimized for their customer (enterprise sales leaders who want dashboards). the smaller tool is optimized for your actual job (landing in the inbox).

before you renew your outreach contract, ask what you're actually paying for. if the answer is 'the dashboards' you're overpaying.

what other 'default' tools are you paying for that have better alternatives?

remixes.hook: the legacy outbound tools have a quiet problem. their best customers are slowly being unbundled by 4-tool stacks costing 60% less.
remixes.body:
killed our outreach subscription 4 months ago. replaced it with instantly, clay, and a zapier loop. sending 3x the volume at 40% better deliverability for 60% less spend.

the tool itself was fine. that's not what triggered the cut. what triggered it was realizing that outreach was bundling four jobs into one expensive seat license, and three of those jobs were being done better elsewhere.

bundle one: sending. instantly does this for $97 a month with unlimited sending and better inbox warming. outreach charges per seat.

bundle two: enrichment and list building. clay is the category leader at $349 a month. outreach has nothing close.

bundle three: workflow automation. zapier handles the plumbing for $50 a month and is more flexible than any built-in sequence builder.

bundle four: the actual cadence engine. instantly covers this too.

total new stack: $496 a month. previous stack: $540 a month for three seats, before any add-ons.

the category-killer math is not subtle. legacy bundles are losing to focused tools that win on their one job and integrate cleanly. the next 18 months will be brutal for any sales tool that justifies its price by counting features instead of outcomes.

when we audited our outreach setup, the seat price was the smallest line item.

the expensive part was the deliverability decay. shared sending IPs, aggressive cadence templates, no native inbox warming, and rep behavior that drifted toward spammy because the tool made it easy. our open rates had been sliding for two quarters and we'd written it off as 'the market.'

switched to instantly four months ago. it ships with dedicated warming, IP rotation, and per-mailbox sending limits that you can't accidentally violate. open rates climbed from 31% to 44% in eight weeks. zero copy changes. the tool simply stopped sabotaging the work.

total stack we run now: instantly for sending, clay for enrichment and list building, zapier for the plumbing between them. monthly spend dropped from $540 to $496 while volume tripled.

the lesson is not that outreach is bad. the lesson is that the price of a tool is whatever you pay plus whatever it silently costs you in pipeline. for outbound tools in 2026, deliverability is the line item you should be auditing first.

posts.title: SDR to GTM engineer pipeline
posts.hook: by 2027 every b2b company above $10m arr will have a 'gtm engineer' role. most of the best ones will come from sdr backgrounds, not engineering.
posts.body:
by 2027 every b2b company above $10m arr will have a 'gtm engineer' role. most of the best ones will come from sdr backgrounds, not engineering.

the prediction sounds counterintuitive. gtm engineer sounds like a role for ex-data engineers who learned sales. the reality is the opposite. the best gtm engineers i know are ex-sdrs who learned python.

why: the job isn't about writing production code. the job is translating gtm pain into systems. that translation only works if you've lived the pain.

an ex-data engineer sees 'lead scoring' and builds an elegant model with 47 features. an ex-sdr sees 'lead scoring' and knows which 3 features actually predict a meeting. the sdr ships something useful in a week. the data engineer ships something perfect in 6 months that nobody uses.

the tooling collapsed the skill gap. clay, n8n, cursor, claude api. you don't need a cs degree to build gtm infrastructure anymore. you need taste for what to build, which only comes from having done the work.

if you're an sdr right now and you're not learning sql + basic python + clay + an llm api, you're leaving career runway on the table. the gtm engineering role is the biggest pay bump available in gtm and it rewards operators who learned to build.

sdrs: what's stopping you from making the jump?
posts.cta: sdrs: what's stopping you from making the jump?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering principles into go-to-market strategies. Emphasizes data-driven experimentation and automation to accelerate revenue growth. Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing teams.

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: everyone thinks gtm engineer is a role for ex-engineers who learned sales. wrong direction. the best ones are ex-sdrs who learned python.
remixes.body:
every vc deck i read in 2026 talks about 'gtm engineer' like it's a new engineering track. it isn't.

the role sits at the intersection of three things: pipeline context, buyer psychology, and just enough code to wire clay, claude, and hubspot together. engineers can learn the last one in a weekend. they can't learn the first two without 18 months of cold calls under their belt.

watched a series b company hire a staff engineer into this role in january. smart kid, stanford cs. built a gorgeous scoring pipeline. the scores were useless because he didn't know what a 'good fit' account looked like. they replaced him with an sdr who'd been at the company 2 years and could barely write a for loop. pipeline quality tripled in 6 weeks.

the moat isn't the code. the moat is knowing which 400 accounts out of 40,000 are worth the company's attention this quarter. sdrs have that context burned into their nervous system. engineers treat it as a data problem.

by 2027, the title exists at every $10m+ b2b company. 70% of the hires will come from sdr, ae, or rev ops backgrounds. the rest will wash out.
remixes.cta: if you're an sdr reading this: start with a clay cookbook this weekend.

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: here's the exact 6-month path from sdr to gtm engineer i've seen work at 4 companies.
remixes.body:
the gtm engineer role is about to explode and sdrs have the best seat at the table. not because of ambition. because of context.

month 1-2: learn clay properly. not 'i used it once.' build 3 enrichment workflows end to end. understand waterfalls, credits, and why 60% of the data you pay for is junk.

month 3: learn basic python through data tasks only. pandas, requests, a little sqlite. skip leetcode. your job is moving rows between systems, not building compilers.

month 4: wire claude or gpt into one clay table. start with account classification. then move to personalization. learn what a prompt that works in production looks like.

month 5: replace a tool in your stack with a custom pipeline. most teams have an enrichment vendor costing $30k+ that you can replace with apify + claude for $400/mo. this is your portfolio piece.

month 6: write up what you built, share it internally, ask to own the pipeline for one segment. you are now a gtm engineer, whether hr agrees yet or not.

the sdrs who do this in 2026 will be making $180k+ by 2027. the ones who don't will be competing with 10x more sdrs for the same seats.

sdrs keep getting told they need to 'upskill into engineering' to get out of the bdr pit. nope. you need to upskill into clay, claude, and sql. that's it. the companies paying $180k+ for this role right now are not looking for fellows at a bootcamp. they're looking for someone who can still quote their icp definition from memory while also shipping a working enrichment pipeline by friday.

posts.title: Debugging enrichment chains
posts.hook: burned $380 in clay credits tuesday on a workflow that didn't work. the debugging post-mortem taught me more than building it did.
posts.body:
burned $380 in clay credits tuesday on a workflow that didn't work. the debugging post-mortem taught me more than building it did.

the workflow: enrich 1400 companies with firmographic data, run them through an llm classifier, score 1-5, push the 4s and 5s into hubspot.

what happened: llm returned scores for every row. hubspot received 1400 companies with score '3'. every single one. a perfect bell curve collapsed to a single value.

first instinct: blame the llm. wrong.

the bug was in the column before the classifier. i was passing the company description field, but the enrichment source had inconsistent fields. 40% of rows had 'description', 60% had 'about'. the prompt saw 'description: [empty]' for 60% of rows and defaulted to 'unclear, score 3'.

the lesson has nothing to do with clay. it's about enrichment chain debugging. when an agent produces weird output, walk the chain backwards one step at a time. check what the agent actually received, not what you think it received. 9 times out of 10 the problem is upstream of the agent.

built a debug column that captures the raw input to every ai step. now when something goes sideways i can see exactly what the llm was staring at when it made a bad call.

what debugging habits have you built for ai workflows?
posts.cta: what debugging habits have you built for ai workflows?

remixes.label: lessons-extracted
remixes.hook: set $380 on fire in clay credits tuesday. here are the 5 guardrails i'm adding to every ai workflow from now on.
remixes.body:
tuesday morning: kicked off a workflow to enrich 1400 companies, run them through an llm classifier, score 1-5, route 4s and 5s to hubspot. walked away to take a call. came back to a $380 credit burn and every single company scored exactly 3.

the post-mortem is more valuable than the workflow would have been. guardrails i'm adding to every workflow from now on:

1. test on 10 rows first. always. the cost of running 10 is $2.70. the cost of running 1400 is $380. there is no universe where skipping the 10-row test is correct.

2. sample the output distribution. if every row returns the same value, something upstream is broken. a simple histogram check catches this before it hits production.

3. log the raw llm response, not just the parsed score. when you parse '3' out of 'i cannot score this company because...' you deserve what you get.

4. set a hard credit ceiling per workflow. clay lets you cap spend. most people don't use it because it feels like a lack of confidence. the confidence is expensive.

5. never kick off a long run and walk away on the first execution. watch the first 50 rows. if they look wrong, kill it. if they look right, go get coffee.

most expensive lesson i've learned all year. cheapest one i'll ever pass on.
remixes.cta: which guardrail would you add?

remixes.label: what-went-wrong
remixes.hook: the actual reason my clay workflow scored every company '3' out of 5 wasn't the llm. it was a bug i should've caught in 90 seconds.
remixes.body:
spent 40 minutes blaming claude before i opened the raw response payload. the llm was fine. my prompt was fine. the data was the problem, and it was stupid.

here's what happened: i had an upstream enrichment step that was supposed to pull firmographic data (employee count, funding stage, industry). that step was silently failing. not erroring out, just returning empty strings for 90% of companies.

the llm was getting prompts that looked like:
'score this company on a 1-5 scale based on fit:
company name: acme corp
employees: 
funding: 
industry: 
website: '

and it was doing the most reasonable thing possible: refusing to commit to a score with no data and defaulting to the middle. '3. insufficient information to score higher or lower.'

i would have done the same thing.

the fix was 2 lines of validation: if the enrichment fields are empty, skip the llm call entirely. don't send it a pointless prompt. don't let it default. route those rows to a 'needs manual review' bucket.

the lesson: when an llm gives you weird output, the prompt and the model are rarely the problem. the context you're feeding it almost always is. debug upstream before you debug the model.

the lesson has nothing to do with clay. it's about enrichment chain debugging. when an agent produces weird output, walk the chain backwards one step at a time. check what the agent actually received, not what you think it received. 9 times out of 10 the problem is upstream of the agent.

built a debug column that captures the raw input to every ai step. now when something goes sideways i can see exactly what the llm was staring at when it made a bad call.

what debugging habits have you built for ai workflows?
tones.advisor:
i want to share something expensive so you don't have to repeat it.

tuesday i ran a clay workflow that cost $380 and produced zero usable output. enriched 1400 companies, passed them to an llm classifier, every single one came back scored '3' on a 1-5 scale. perfect bell curve of uselessness.

my recommended protocol, after 3 years of running ai workflows in production:

first, never run a new workflow at full volume. run 10 rows. inspect every output by eye. if it looks right, run 100. inspect again. then and only then go to 1400. the marginal cost of this discipline is 15 minutes. the cost of skipping it is what i paid tuesday.

second, instrument your distributions. if your scoring rubric is 1-5 and every row returns the same value, your workflow is broken, full stop. a simple count-by-score check would have caught this in the first 20 rows.

third, separate the llm call from the data layer. 90% of the time you think you have an llm problem, you have a missing-data problem. my prompts were fine. my upstream enrichment was silently failing and feeding the llm empty strings.

the habit i'd recommend building: treat every new ai workflow like a prod deploy. dry run, canary, full rollout. same discipline. same boring checklist. it's the boring checklist that saves you $380.

everyone posting about their 'agentic workflows' on this app has not shipped a single one to production. i can tell because they don't have the scar tissue.

tuesday i burned $380 in clay credits on a workflow that scored 1400 companies as exactly '3'. the same middle number. every single one. a perfect monument to me not checking my work.

you know what happens when you post about this kind of failure? the engagement-farming consultants show up to tell you 'great lessons learned' and then go back to posting their imaginary agent stacks that have never moved a dollar of revenue.

here's the part nobody shares: ai workflows fail silently and expensively. the model doesn't error. it doesn't flag uncertainty. it confidently produces garbage and your credit card processes the charge. by the time you notice, you've already shipped 1400 rows of junk into hubspot and now sales is working a list that was never vetted.

the operators treating llms like reliable functions are going to get clobbered by this in 2026. the ones treating them like interns who hallucinate when they're underfed will survive.

if you haven't set money on fire yet, you haven't shipped anything real. and if you have shipped and haven't, you're not paying attention to your logs.

posts.title: Enterprise stack getting eaten
posts.hook: the enterprise gtm stack is getting eaten by tools that cost less than your morning coffee. clay at $149/mo is doing what 6sense charges $80k/year for.
posts.body:
the enterprise gtm stack is getting eaten by tools that cost less than your morning coffee. clay at $149/mo is doing what 6sense charges $80k/year for.

specific examples from the last year:
- clay replaced zoominfo + bombora intent in 2 customer stacks i worked on
- instantly replaced outreach at 4 companies i know personally
- apify + claude api replaced a $120k/year gartner subscription for market research
- n8n replaced zapier + tray.io at a 50-person series b

the pattern: enterprise tools charged for features. bootstrapped tools charge for usage. when usage is cheap, the bundled feature pricing becomes indefensible.

this isn't about enterprise tools being bad. they're not. the pricing model is bad. they priced for a world where building software required a team. now it requires a prompt.

every enterprise gtm tool has a 'your job in a box' pricing page. $80k/year to replace an intern. a gtm engineer with clay + a claude api key builds the same capability in a weekend for $500/month.

the real question is which enterprise tools figure out usage-based pricing before the cheaper alternatives eat their mid-market.

which enterprise tool in your stack are you watching most closely?

remixes.label: market-collapse
remixes.hook: the enterprise gtm vendor pricing model is collapsing in slow motion and most founders can't see it yet. 3 categories gone in 18 months.
remixes.body:
the $80k/year enterprise contract for intent data is already dead. the contracts just haven't been canceled yet.

watched this play out in 3 categories over the last 18 months:

intent data: 6sense, bombora, g2 signals. pricing anchored at $60k-$150k/year. replaced by clay at $149/mo plus a handful of apis (builtwith, news triggers, job postings via apify). 95% of the functional output for 0.4% of the cost.

sales engagement: outreach and salesloft at $130+ per seat per month. replaced by instantly at $37/mo plus smartlead for deliverability. 4 companies i know personally made this swap in 2025. none have gone back.

market intelligence: gartner and forrester subscriptions at $40k-$120k/year. replaced by claude api running over apify scrapes of the same analyst reports + earnings calls + reddit + g2 reviews. one prompt chain, $200/mo in api costs, better latency.

the enterprise sales motion that sold these tools was 18 month cycles, legal reviews, msas, annual renewals. the replacement motion is a credit card, a sunday afternoon, and a clay template from youtube.

the enterprise tools aren't getting cheaper. their buyers are getting smarter and their alternatives are getting better fast enough that price discipline no longer holds.
remixes.cta: which enterprise vendor do you think is next?

remixes.hook: i replaced a $240k/year enterprise gtm stack for a series b client in 6 weeks. new total cost: $8,400/year. here's the full swap.
remixes.body:
client was spending $240k/year on what they called 'the gtm stack.' six weeks of work and the number is $8,400/year. same output. better data. faster iteration. here's the full swap.

out: zoominfo enterprise ($72k/year). in: clay at $149/mo with a waterfall of apollo, anymailfinder, and prospeo. monthly cost $1,788.

out: 6sense intent platform ($85k/year). in: clay + bombora api direct + custom signals from apify scrapes of careers pages and podcast mentions. monthly cost $400.

out: outreach sales engagement ($47k/year across 22 seats). in: instantly at $97/mo for unlimited sends plus smartlead for inbox rotation. monthly cost $297.

out: gartner research subscription ($36k/year). in: claude api running a weekly research workflow over earnings calls, g2 reviews, and industry podcasts. monthly cost $215.

total new monthly: $700. total new annual: $8,400. total saved: $231,600.

the hidden cost: 6 weeks of a gtm engineer's time. call it $18k all-in. still a 12x return in year one, and the pipeline keeps compounding because the new stack is owned, not rented.

the biggest pushback was cultural, not technical. two directors had been promoted on the back of buying those enterprise tools. swapping them felt like deleting their résumé.

for founders and heads of revenue evaluating gtm tools in 2026, my guidance is direct: stop signing annual enterprise contracts until you've run a 90-day bootstrapped pilot.

the enterprise gtm stack is being eaten by tools that cost less than your team's coffee budget. clay at $149/mo is doing what 6sense charges $80k/year for. instantly at $97/mo is replacing outreach deployments that cost $50k+. apify plus claude api is replacing gartner subscriptions at a fraction of the cost and with faster turnaround.

i'm not suggesting every enterprise tool is overpriced. i'm suggesting the default purchasing motion (rfp, legal review, annual contract, auto-renew) is badly mispriced against a world where a gtm engineer with a credit card can build 80% of the capability in 6 weeks.

concrete recommendation: before your next annual renewal, run a parallel pilot. give one of your revops or sdr folks 6 weeks and $1,500/mo in tooling budget. ask them to reproduce the top 3 workflows the enterprise tool powers. measure output quality and speed against the incumbent.

i have run this exercise with 8 clients in the last year. in 7 of them, the bootstrapped pilot matched or exceeded the enterprise tool on the workflows the team actually used. in the 8th, the enterprise tool won on one specific capability (deep firmographic depth for fortune 500), so they downgraded the contract instead of canceling.

nobody regrets running the pilot. plenty regret signing the renewal without one.

the enterprise gtm vendors know they're cooked. that's why the sales motion has gotten more aggressive, not less, over the last 18 months. longer contracts, bigger discounts for multi-year lock-ins, free seats if you sign by end of quarter. classic late-stage monopolist behavior.

clay at $149/mo is doing what 6sense charges $80k/year for. not 'roughly equivalent.' functionally equivalent. i've seen the side by side runs at 4 companies.

the reason your competitor is paying 1/500th of what you are is that they hired a 26-year-old ex-sdr who figured out clay on youtube and built the workflow in 3 weekends. the reason you're still paying $80k is that your vp of rev ops got promoted on the back of buying 6sense and cancelling the contract would be admitting that the promotion wasn't about tool selection, it was about vendor relationships.

gartner at $120k/year is being replaced by claude api running over scraped earnings calls and reddit threads. the analysts at gartner are doing the exact same thing with a longer turnaround and a prettier pdf.

if you're a founder signing a 3-year enterprise gtm contract in 2026, you are voluntarily subsidizing a business model that won't exist by 2028. the consultants will tell you it's about 'enterprise-grade security' and 'support slas.' it's about the sales rep's daughter's college tuition.

posts.title: 5-part outbound scoring rubric
posts.hook: 5-part scoring rubric i run every outbound list through before it hits the sending tool. built from 3 years of tracking what actually converts.
posts.body:
5-part scoring rubric i run every outbound list through before it hits the sending tool. built from 3 years of tracking what actually converts.

1. role velocity: are they hiring into the function i'm selling to? pulled from their careers page via clay. 1-5 scale. anything under 3 gets dropped.

2. recency of last funding: closed rounds in the last 18 months correlate with 3x higher reply rates. not because they have money. because new funding means new priorities.

3. stack signals: are they running a competing tool? builtwith + apollo tech stack data. if yes, bump score. if they just signed a 2-year contract with a competitor, drop score.

4. champion persona match: one specific human at the account who maps to our usual buyer archetype. title + tenure + past company history. if no clear champion, the account ranks as speculative.

5. content engagement: has anyone at the company engaged with our linkedin posts, podcast, or website in the last 60 days? warm accounts convert at 4x cold accounts.

each row gets a score 1-5 on each dimension. 25 max. anything under 15 doesn't get sent to. anything over 20 goes to manual outbound with a custom first line.

the rubric took 6 iterations to land. first 3 versions overweighted firmographics. the breakthrough was weighting behavioral signals higher than firmographic ones. a small company that engaged with your content last week outperforms a perfectly fit company that never heard of you.

full scorecard and clay columns in the comments.

what's in your scoring model?

inspired_by.summary: Shares insights on revenue operations as the backbone of high-growth SaaS companies. Details how RevOps unifies sales, marketing, and customer success through shared KPIs and tech stacks. Includes case studies from scaling startups.

remixes.label: full-rubric
remixes.hook: the 5-part scoring rubric that killed 62% of my outbound list last quarter and doubled my reply rate. exact thresholds below.
remixes.body:
built this over 3 years of tracking what actually converts. every outbound list goes through it before a single email sends. last quarter it killed 62% of the starting list and doubled my reply rate on what remained.

1. role velocity. are they hiring into the function i sell to? pulled from careers page via clay. 1-5 scale based on count and recency. anything under 3 gets dropped. a company not hiring into your buyer function is signaling 'not a priority right now.'

2. recency of last funding. closed rounds in the last 18 months correlate with 3x higher reply rates in my data. no funding data or last round over 36 months old = score 1. under 6 months = score 5.

3. tech stack signal. are they using one of 4 specific tools that indicate buying readiness for what i sell? pulled from builtwith and apify-scraped job descriptions. binary. either the signal exists or it doesn't.

4. engagement footprint. has anyone from this company engaged with my content, my company's content, or adjacent content in the last 90 days? pulled from linkedin company pages via apify and rb2b pixel data. ties directly to reply rate.

5. stated pain match. does their website, job postings, or recent content mention a problem i solve, in their own words? this is the killer. the list drops 40% on this alone. but the replies from what remains convert at 4x the rate of the rest.

minimum 15/25 composite. drop everything else.
remixes.cta: which signal are you not capturing yet?

remixes.label: philosophy-flip
remixes.hook: i used to score outbound lists on 15 dimensions. cut it to 5 last year and my reply rate went up, not down. more signal isn't better. right signal is better.
remixes.body:
for two years i built scoring models with 15+ signals. firmographic depth, technographic layers, intent sources from 4 different vendors, engagement across 6 channels, industry-specific enrichment. the dashboards were gorgeous. the reply rates were mediocre.

last year i cut the model to 5 signals and my reply rate went up 40%. the insight: more signals don't make lists better. they make them noisier. every signal you add introduces false positives and weighting complexity. past a certain point you're optimizing the model's elegance, not the pipeline's output.

the 5 that survived:

role velocity (are they hiring into my buyer function). recency of funding (18 months or less). tech stack signal (4 specific tools that indicate readiness). engagement footprint (any touch with me, my company, or adjacent content in 90 days). stated pain match (their own words, on their careers page or blog, describing the problem i solve).

that's it. five dimensions. each scored 1-5. composite of 15 or higher moves forward.

the signals i cut: industry, headcount tier, revenue band, geographic region, technology category, executive tenure, product launch recency, press mentions, investor quality, board composition. most of these correlate weakly with reply rate at best and add weighting noise at worst.

the rubric is the discipline. 5 dimensions force you to pick the ones that actually predict outcomes instead of the ones that look impressive in a slide deck.

tones.advisor:
if you're running outbound and you don't have a formal scoring rubric, my recommendation is to build one before you send another sequence.

here's the framework i recommend to every gtm team i advise, refined over 3 years of tracking reply rates against list composition.

first, role velocity. are they hiring into the function you sell to? pull from careers page data via clay. score 1-5 based on count and recency. my guidance: drop anything scoring under 3. a company not hiring into your buyer function is implicitly telling you it's not a priority.

second, recency of last funding. closed rounds in the last 18 months correlate with 3x higher reply rates in my data. if funding data is missing or the last round is over 36 months old, score 1. under 6 months, score 5.

third, tech stack signals. identify the 3-5 specific tools that indicate buying readiness for your product. pull from builtwith or job descriptions via apify. binary presence, score 0 or 5.

fourth, engagement footprint. any touch with your content, your company, or adjacent content in the last 90 days. this is the highest-leverage signal most teams under-instrument.

fifth, stated pain match. does their website, careers page, or recent public content describe the problem you solve in their own words? this is the most effective filter and the most overlooked.

composite threshold: 15 of 25. drop the rest. trust the rubric.
tones.provocateur:
most outbound lists are insulting. someone paid for a 20,000 row zoominfo export, filtered by revenue band and industry, and called it a strategy. then they wonder why reply rates are below 1%.

you do not have a list problem. you have a list discipline problem.

here is i've been screaming at teams for 3 years: 5 signals, scored rigorously, will beat 15 signals weighted by vibes every time. the 5 that matter:

role velocity. is the company actively hiring into the function you sell to? if not, delete the row. they are telling you, in public, that this is not a priority. take the hint.

funding recency. 18 months or less. after that, budget psychology calcifies and you're selling against a deferred renewal.

tech stack signal. 4 specific tools. either present or not. binary. no soft scoring.

engagement footprint. if nobody at the company has ever touched your orbit, you are cold prospecting, and cold prospecting in 2026 is a waste of everyone's time and your send reputation.

stated pain match. their words, not yours. if their careers page doesn't describe the problem you solve, you're projecting.

anyone can buy a list. the job is to throw most of it away. the reps who will survive 2026 are the ones who delete 80% of their targets before the first email and double their reply rate on what remains.

posts.title: Data layer before agents
posts.hook: teams shipping ai agents without a clean data layer are building on sand. the agent is never the bottleneck. the context is.
posts.body:
teams shipping ai agents without a clean data layer are building on sand. the agent is never the bottleneck. the context is.

watched a company spend 4 months building a 'customer success agent' that was supposed to analyze account health and draft qbr prep. the agent was beautiful. claude sonnet, tool use, structured outputs, the full stack.

it failed because their customer data lived in 7 places. hubspot had contracts. intercom had support. mixpanel had product usage. notion had qbr notes. salesforce had revenue. stripe had billing. segment had activity.

the agent couldn't see the customer. it could see 7 partial views and a prompt telling it to synthesize. it guessed. the guesses were wrong.

the fix wasn't a better agent. the fix was a data warehouse + dbt models that unified all 7 sources into one 'customer' table before the agent ran. once the agent had one clean table, it got competent overnight.

the industry is about to learn this the expensive way. every vendor is selling 'agents'. almost nobody is selling the data foundation agents actually need. teams buy the agent, skip the foundation, get mediocre output, blame the agent.

if you're greenlighting an agent project and your data is scattered across 5+ tools with no warehouse, build the warehouse first. 4 weeks of plumbing saves 4 months of debugging.

what does your data layer look like before the agent runs?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Nat breaks down recent advancements in AI infrastructure for agent deployment, focusing on cost-effective scaling with providers like Grok and Together AI. He analyzes automation trends and tools for building reliable agent fleets. Shares data on engagement metrics for agent-powered apps.

remixes.label: sand-foundation
remixes.hook: watched a company spend 4 months and $600k building a 'customer success agent' that shipped broken. the agent was beautiful. their data was a dumpster fire.
remixes.body:
this happens constantly in 2026 and nobody is talking about it honestly. the agent is never the bottleneck. the context is.

company i worked with last quarter. series c, 180 employees, solid engineering team. spent 4 months building a customer success agent that was supposed to analyze account health and draft qbr prep docs. claude sonnet under the hood, tool use wired up properly, structured outputs, full observability stack. the agent worked. the agent was gorgeous.

it failed because their customer data lived in 7 places. hubspot had lifecycle stage. stripe had payment history. zendesk had ticket volume. mixpanel had product usage. salesforce had deal history. a shared google sheet had 'strategic notes' from the csm team that never made it into any system. and crucially, no single place had current arr per customer.

the agent was prompted to synthesize account health. it got 7 contradictory inputs, each with a different definition of 'customer,' 'status,' and 'revenue.' it produced a beautifully written qbr document that was 40% wrong on the numbers.

cs team lost trust in the tool by week 3. by week 8 the champion internally had been assigned other projects. by month 4 it was decommissioned. $600k burned. the engineering team wasn't wrong about the agent. leadership was wrong about the sequencing.

data layer first. always. no exceptions. the agent is the last 10% of the problem, not the first 90%.
remixes.cta: what would your agent look like if you fixed the data layer first?

remixes.label: sequencing-rule
remixes.hook: rule i give every team starting an ai agent project: 6 months on the data layer before 1 line of agent code. pushback is constant. the teams who listen ship. the teams who don't, don't.
remixes.body:
i've watched 12 companies attempt to ship production ai agents in the last 18 months. 4 succeeded. 8 failed. the single factor separating the two groups wasn't model choice, prompt quality, team size, or even budget. it was sequencing.

the 4 that succeeded spent 6+ months on the data layer before writing agent code. the 8 that failed started with the agent.

what 'data layer' actually means in this context:

1. one source of truth per entity. if you ask 'what is our arr for acme corp,' there should be one answer, one system, one query. not 3 different numbers across stripe, salesforce, and a google sheet.

2. entity resolution that works. 'acme corp' in hubspot and 'acme corporation' in salesforce and 'acme.com' in your product db all need to resolve to the same canonical id. without this, every agent output is a half-baked merge of contradictory inputs.

3. event history, not just current state. you need to know when a customer moved from trial to paid, not just that they are currently paid. agents reasoning about 'change' need temporal data, and most cs and revops stacks don't have it.

4. a semantic layer. structured definitions of what 'active,' 'at risk,' 'expansion ready' actually mean in your business, queryable by the agent.

until those 4 things exist, the agent is a demo, not a product. i know the 6-month number sounds unreasonable. the 4-month failed build is less reasonable.
remixes.cta: which of the 4 does your team not have yet?

tones.builder:
teams shipping ai agents without a clean data layer are building on sand. the agent is never the bottleneck. the context is.

watched a company spend 4 months building a 'customer success agent' that was supposed to analyze account health and draft qbr prep. the agent was beautiful. claude sonnet, tool use, structured outputs, the full stack.

it failed because their customer data lived in 7 places. hubspot had contracts. intercom had support. mixpanel had product usage. notion had qbr notes. salesforce had revenue. stripe had billing. segment had activity.

the agent couldn't see the customer. it could see 7 partial views and a prompt telling it to synthesize. it guessed. the guesses were wrong.

the fix wasn't a better agent. the fix was a data warehouse + dbt models that unified all 7 sources into one 'customer' table before the agent ran. once the agent had one clean table, it got competent overnight.

the industry is about to learn this the expensive way. every vendor is selling 'agents'. almost nobody is selling the data foundation agents actually need. teams buy the agent, skip the foundation, get mediocre output, blame the agent.

if you're greenlighting an agent project and your data is scattered across 5+ tools with no warehouse, build the warehouse first. 4 weeks of plumbing saves 4 months of debugging.

what does your data layer look like before the agent runs?
tones.advisor:
before you approve another ai agent project, i want to give you one piece of advice that will save you 6 figures: spend 3 months auditing your data layer before you write a single line of agent code.

the agent is never the bottleneck. the context is. i've watched 12 ai agent builds in the last 18 months and the pattern is ruthlessly consistent. teams that invested in the data layer first shipped working agents. teams that started with the agent shipped beautiful failures.

here's what i recommend the pre-agent audit cover.

first, source of truth mapping. for every entity your agent will reason about (customer, account, deal, user), identify the single canonical system of record. if there isn't one, building one is your actual project, not the agent.

second, entity resolution. can you answer 'are these two records the same company' deterministically across your stack? if not, any agent you build will produce merged outputs from contradictory inputs and your team will lose trust in it within weeks.

third, event history. does your data layer capture state transitions over time, not just current state? agents asked to reason about 'health' or 'risk' or 'change' need temporal context.

fourth, a semantic layer. structured, queryable definitions of what 'at risk,' 'expansion ready,' 'power user' actually mean in your business.

skip this audit and you will ship a beautiful agent that fails. do the audit and the agent becomes the last 10% of the problem, which is how it should be.
tones.provocateur:
the 'ai agent' demos filling your linkedin feed are lying to you. not because the demos are fake. because the demos run on clean data that exists nowhere in your actual company.

every ai agent project i've watched fail in the last 18 months, and there are a lot, failed for the same reason. the team started with the agent because the agent was fun. the data layer was boring. the agent ran on top of a data layer that had 7 sources of truth, inconsistent entity resolution, and no event history. the outputs were confidently wrong. the team lost trust in 6 weeks. the project was quietly killed by month 4.

if you are a head of cs, rev ops, or product thinking about shipping an ai agent this year and your data lives in 7 systems with no canonical definitions, you do not have an agent project. you have a data infrastructure project pretending to be an agent project.

the vendors selling you 'agents in a weekend' know this. they are not going to tell you because the check clears before the agent breaks. by the time you figure out the real problem, they are 3 customers down the road.

build the data layer. it's boring. it's unglamorous. it's the only reason any ai agent in production works. the teams who skip it ship demos. the teams who don't ship products.
